- Renovations done through single person contractors. They hire more help when they need it.
- Contractors have tight timelines. Do not have flexibility to do huge jobs with new technology
- When designing a big project (renovating kitchen or bathroom), work starts with a designer
- Making designs is a collaborative project. Upselling is common when it comes to nice materials or newer appliances
- A larger renovator includes Royal Renovations. They may be more interested

- When discussing possible uses, the most common ones are:
1) Peace of mind through additional security (motion sensors, video cameras, automatic lights). Not a huge selling point in a small, safe town
2) Time savings. Automated window blinds or automatic wall outlet timers could save time from my mom's routine of adjusting window blinds or using xmas lights

Interesting point:
Home automation already exists for significant pain points including: 
1) Garage door openers
2) Mechanical wall outlet timers
3) Light dimmers (not really automated as more flexible control)
A home automation solution must some additional value over the existing solutions.

-Seasonal trends in tools (power washer in the summer)
-No budget for marketting. The biggest thing preventing growth is spreading the word and they only have word-of-mouth right now
-Roughly $50k in revenue and $50k in expenses for 2016
-This is a huge increase from previous year (2015 they we around $35k for both
-2k members
-They host regular workshops
-Mainly use two software packages, one for accounts and one for payments. I did not get their names :(
This is an amazing widgit that will blow everyone off the ground! The value proposition is to solve the painpoint of all consumers!
Tool libraries are sprining up across the country and growing in popularity. What are the biggest problems they face and is there space for a product that could help them improve?
Sensors are cheap. Managing big data is cheap. Why aren't more people adopting this in their homes?
